data.name: Utkir Yusupov
data.description: Yusupov was born in Kazakhstan’s Turkistan region and moved to Tashkent with his family when he was five. He started his footballing life as a midfielder, but his team’s goalkeeper kept making mistakes so he took over the position – and stayed there. To support his family, he also worked in a shop and sold pastries in a market. “I didn’t have money to buy gloves. I would buy construction workers’ gloves at the market and wear two layers,” he recalled. In 2017, Yusupov obtained Uzbek citizenship and made his debut for the national team a year later. “I debuted in a friendly against Qatar,” he recalled. “It unexpectedly snowed in October, and I went out wearing a short-sleeved kit. I was freezing the entire match – but I didn’t concede a goal.” Yusupov is currently the first-choice goalkeeper for the national team, with excellent reflexes, and is considered a penalty-saving expert, unusually adept at reading his opponent's intentions. He saved two penalties in the 2026 World Cup qualifiers.

data.name: Abdukodir Khusanov
data.description: The first Uzbek player to play in the English Premier League and France’s Ligue 1. Khusanov's father, Hikmat Khoshimov, also played for the national team at centre-back. At the age of 17 Khusanov signed for Energetik-BGU in Belarus, where he spent one difficult year. "In Belarus, I suffered being away from my parents,” he said. “Often there were training sessions three times a day. If there was heavy snow in winter, we would clear the field ourselves and then train." Khusanov’s career was transformed in 2023: he became an Asian champion as part of Uzbekistan Under-20s in March, participated in the Under-20 World Cup in May, made his debut for the senior team in June, moved to Lens in July and became the youngest Uzbek player to appear in the Champions League in November. In January 2025 Khusanov moved to Manchester City. "This is one of the best transfers in the history of the club," said Pep Guardiola. "He does not shy away from learning, takes training seriously and works with all his might. In short, Abdukodir is a very reliable player."

data.name: Khojiakbar Alijonov
data.description: Alijonov was taken to football training by his parents at the age of six. His father had also played, but never reached the top level. Alijonov initially trained with boys three years older than him. “There were no age groups for players my age, so I had to train with the ones born in 1994,” he recalled. In 2005, when he was eight, he joined Pakhtakor’s academy, and he has now been at the club for 21 years without interruption. Today, he holds the Uzbek league record for playing continuously for the same club. He took his first steps in the academy as a forward, which is why he loves the No 7 shirt. In 2017 he made his debut for Pakhtakor’s first team at left-back, and has made more than 230 appearances and won the Uzbekistan championship five times, the national cup on three occasions and the Super Cup twice. He joined the national team in 2019 and has been in it long enough to appreciate the significance of their World Cup qualification. “As soon as the match against the UAE ended, I couldn’t hold back my tears right there on the pitch,” he says.

data.name: Farrukh Sayfiev
data.description: At 35, Sayfiev is established as one of the country's finest ever full-backs. He is known as Mr Stability in Uzbek football. Born in Samarkand, Sayfiev has spent most of his career with two clubs, with seven years at Nasaf followed by six at Pakhtakor. With these teams he won five Uzbek championships – a sixth followed last year, at Neftchi – two Uzbek Cups, and four Uzbek Super Cups. Sayfiev was once asked at which of these two clubs he would choose to end his career. "I became famous at Nasaf, and I developed into a strong player at Pakhtakor,” he said. “It's very difficult to choose between them." Sayfiev, who gave the contents of his first pay packet as a footballer entirely to his mother, is often compared to Vitaly Denisov, perhaps the best left-back in the history of the Uzbekistan team – by others, at least. "You have to be realistic,” the player himself says. “Denisov is better than me." If he sounds generous and compassionate, he does not always play that way: fuelled by an unbreaking desire to win he has received 80 yellow cards and five reds in his career.

data.name: Rustam Ashurmatov
data.description: Ashurmatov is the nephew of the famous former international centre-back Bakhtiyor Ashurmatov, but through a combination of hard work and skill he has been able to step out of his uncle’s shadow and make his own reputation. He has developed into a solid defender for the Uzbekistan team and, after Khusniddin Alikulov's injury, is expected to form the first-choice central defensive partnership with Abdukodir Khusanov. Ashurmatov is a product of the Tashkent club Bunyodkor, and played in South Korea and Russia before joining his current side, Esteghlal, in 2025 after catching the eye of the Iranian side’s sporting director, Clarence Seedorf, with his ability not only to protect his own goal but to threaten from set pieces. Ashurmatov has ambitious goals for the upcoming World Cup. "We need to play high-quality football no matter who our opponent is. We need to at least advance from the group stage," the defender said. "Our group is very difficult. But we qualified for the World Cup, and only the strongest qualify."

data.name: Akmal Mozgovoy
data.description: At 19, Mozgovoy made his debut for Karshi's Nasaf in the Super League and became a key component of Ruzikul Berdiev's team, going on to play 224 matches for them, winning the Uzbek Cup and Super Cup three times. In 2024 he helped bring the club the long-awaited championship, and it was his goal against Lokomotiv Tashkent that sealed the title. "Remembering that goal, memories from childhood tournaments come back to me,” he said. “I used to score that kind of goal often. After the match, I talked to my grandfather, and he reminded me of it. It was an unforgettable experience." At the end of that season he was named the league’s player of the year, and received offers from teams in Poland, Russia, Cyprus and elsewhere. Instead he chose to stay, and though he joined Baniyas of the United Arab Emirates Pro League six months later he was not happy with the decision and within seven months was back in Uzbekistan, signing for Pakhtakor. He is the kind of player you look to in crunch moments, and when Uzbekistan secured victories on penalties over Iran in the final of the Al Ain Cup in November 2025, and against Venezuela in the final of the Fifa Series tournament in Tashkent in March 2026, it was Mozgovoy who converted the decisive spot-kicks on both occasions.

data.name: Otabek Shukurov
data.description: A graduate of the Mashal academy. Shukurov grew up working hard from childhood, washing cars and making shoes. He was the captain of the Uzbekistan team that won the Under-16 Asian Championship in Iran in 2012, and also played in the Under-17 World Cup the following year – Hoffenheim scouted him there and invited him for a trial, but did not proceed with the signing. He made his debut for the senior national team in 2016 and has become more than a player, a conductor of play from the centre of the field, who also has a wicked long shot. The best years of Shukurov’s career came in the United Arab Emirates with Sharjah, where he spent more than four years, became known as the Uzbek Professor, and made a huge contribution to them winning the league in 2019, for the first time in 15 years. Shukurov does a lot of charity work in his hometown of Kashkadarya, and during the pandemic provided food for 60 families there. “When I saw some footballers were doing the toilet roll challenge I felt bad, because that doesn’t help anyone. So I thought of a new challenge, something which can truly benefit poor people,” he said. “When I saw videos of families smiling and feeling happy, this for me was real happiness.”

data.name: Jamshid Iskanderov
data.description: Iskanderov was fast-tracked into the senior side after a run of impressive performances at the 2013 Under-20 World Cup in Turkey, making his debut that September. He might not quite have reached the level of his idols – “I like the games of Xavi, Andrés Iniesta, David Silva and Lionel Messi,” he said – but he has enjoyed plenty of success, winning the Uzbek league title three times in four years with Pakhtakor between 2012 and 2015 and collecting a fourth winner’s medal with Neftchi in 2024-25. In 2022 he won the Uzbekistan footballer of the year prize after an outstanding season with Navbahor Namangan, even though his club lost out on the title by a point. But for all that he has never been a key member of the Uzbek side, and as of the end of May he had been in the squad for 80 games across nearly 13 years, but started only 17. "Every player, no matter how old they are, is waiting for the list of players called up to the national team. I am waiting too," he says.

data.name: Odiljon Hamrobekov
data.description: An irreplaceable player at the core of the national team: Khamrobekov played all but 16 minutes of Uzbekistan’s 14-game qualifying campaign, and is a guaranteed starter when fit. He also played for his country at various youth levels and in several World Cups and Asian Championships, winning the player of the tournament award at the 2018 Under-23 Asian Championships in China. "That tournament was very memorable, the final was held in heavy snow. It's unforgettable,” he said. He started his career at Nasaf, but after moving to Pakhtakor in 2019 won five successive league titles, a particularly impressive achievement given that in the middle of that run he snuck in half a season with Shabab al-Ahli in the United Arab Emitraes and won a cup there too. In 2025 he moved to Tractor in Iran, where he promptly won another league title. He is considered the engine at the heart of the Uzbekistan team, and is a master at anticipating and ending opponent attacks. He works extremely hard for the team but he has not always been appreciated by fans.

data.name: Ruslanbek Jiyanov
data.description: A late replacement for the injured Jaloliddin Masharipov, Jiyanov made his international debut in 2022 but has not managed to establish himself as a guaranteed pick for his country. Predominantly a left-winger, he can also play on the right and up front. Having joined Pari Nizhniy Novgorod in Russia in 2023, he had two loan spells back in Uzbekistan before signing for Navbahor on a permanent deal at the start of this year. Worked hard to get back from an injury sustained in April and he got his reward with a call-up for the World Cup squad – even if it came late.

data.name: Oston Urunov
data.description: Urunov was born in Navoi, the region named in honor of the great Uzbek poet, thinker, and statesman, the father of the Uzbek literary language, Alisher Navoi. In 2016 Urunov became the captain of the Uzbekistan youth team, made his league debut for Navbahor the following year and in 2018 was named the young player of the year. The award was presented to him by Héctor Cúper, who then gave him his full international debut the following year. At that time, Oston was 18 years and five months old. But after that rapid ascent came a more difficult period: he moved to Ufa in Russia in 2020 and, less than a year later, to Spartak Moscow. "At Spartak everything was going well at first,” he said, “but I couldn't show my full potential because of injury after injury." He spent three years in Russia before returning to Navbahor, but in 2024 was on the move again to Persepolis in Iran, where he won his first league title in 2023-24 – but he still has high ambitions: "Since I was young, I dreamed of playing for Manchester United, and now I dream of PSG," he says.

data.name: Abduvohid Nematov
data.description: Nematov moved from Jizzakh to Qarshi at the age of 14 and joined Nasaf soon afterwards; more than a decade later he is still there, having won every major trophy in Uzbekistan. He is particularly good with his feet, down perhaps to the fact he started in football as a striker, and still has an eye for goal: he scored two of them in the 2023-24 season, both from penalties. "If I was afraid of making a mistake, I wouldn't be a goalkeeper. Because if a goalkeeper makes a mistake, a goal is scored," he says. In 2022 Uzbekistan hosted the Under-23 Asian Cup and Nematov was sent off in the 12th minute of their quarter-final against Iraq, putting the team in a difficult position – and it turned out his mother was watching, having made a secret trip to the stadium. "My mother came to Tashkent to watch the game without telling me that day, and I got sent off. It was terrible," Nematov recalled. In 2024 Uzbekistan qualified for the Olympics for the first time, with Nematov saving a penalty from Sergio Gómez in the match against Spain.

data.name: Sherzod Nasrullaev
data.description: In January, after eight years, 200 games, one league title and three Uzbek cups with Nasaf, Nasrullaev made his first transfer, moving to Tashkent to join Pakhtakor. “This team has helped me grow not only as a player but as a person,” he said. “Every day, every training session, and every game I played for Nasaf will remain an unforgettable memory in my heart. This club’s colours have become part of my life.” None of this would have seemed likely when he was released from their youth system in 2016 at the age of 17, forcing him to drop into the amateur third division. Two years later he was invited back again. Under their tutelage Nasrullaev became a speedy full-back who contributes to attacks and can create dangerous chances with crosses: in 147 league games with Nasaf he scored 10 times and claimed 25 assists, impressive numbers for a defender.

data.name: Eldor Shomurodov
data.description: Uzbekistan’s captain and all-time record goalscorer, and the first Uzbek footballer to score in Italy’s Serie A, Shomurodov was born in 1995 in Jarkurgan district, Uzbekistan’s southernmost region. His father, two uncles, and his older brother were all football players. “I remember we had many cows and sheep at home,” Shomurodov recalled. “All of my friends and cousins loved football. Parents would say: ‘First we tend to the livestock, then we go to the stadium.’” He began attending the local football school at the age of six, and at 12 he moved 180 miles away to Muborak to join Mash’al’s academy. When Rivaldo arrived at Bunyodkor in 2008, his first match in Uzbekistan was played in Muborak against Mash’al – and Shomurodov was the mascot who walked with him on to the pitch. “It was incredible – one of the best players in the world took me on to the field! I will never forget that moment,” he said. Shomurodov grew up as a fan of Chelsea, after being given a kit by an uncle when he was seven. “I always dreamed of meeting Fernando Torres and Didier Drogba and getting their autographs,” he said.

data.name: Umar Eshmurodov
data.description: For fans of the Karshi club Nasaf, Eshmurodov is not just a player but a symbol, the heart of their team. It has not been a straightforward journey – they sold him in 2020, bought him back in 2021, sold him again in 2024 and bought him back once more in 2025 – but along the way he has played more than 300 games for them, winning the Uzbek Cup three times and the Uzbek Supercup twice. The Nasaf head coach, Ruzikul Berdiev, calls the 33-year-old "a coach on the pitch". Eshmurodov made his debut for Uzbekistan in 2020 and has been in the squad for more than 50 matches. He described the team’s World Cup qualification as “the greatest achievement in our history”, adding: “This is not only the dream of our players, but also of the entire Uzbek people. We will give our all in every game when we go on the field to make this dream come true. Our current squad is stronger than ever and we are ready to make history." Eshmurodov is distinguished by his composure on the field, his positioning and his superiority in aerial duels.

data.name: Botirali Ergashev
data.description: Though he showed plenty of early talent, Ergashev’s approach to the game – and with it his career – was transformed after he was selected for the 2015 Under-20 World Cup in New Zealand, where Uzbekistan reached the quarter-finals before losing to Senegal. "After that tournament, my attitude towards football changed radically,” he said. “I was proud to be a footballer. Because you defend the honour of your country, it also awakens the feeling of patriotism in a person." He was also part of the victorious team in the 2018 Under-23 Asian Cup in China. At the beginning of 2024 he moved to Neftchi, and with this team he became the champion of Uzbekistan and a Super Cup winner. He made his debut for the national team in 2018 in a match against Iran but had to wait more than five years to get a second cap, and is still largely restricted to backup duties. "It's nice to wear the national-team jersey. We're trying to live up to it," says Ergashev, who turns 29 on the day of Uzbekistan’s game against Portugal.

data.name: Dostonbek Khamdamov
data.description: An Asian Championship winner at under-16 level in 2012 and at under-23 level in 2018, in between which he was named the best young player in Asia in 2015. All of that success and attention led to a move to the Russian club Anzhi in early 2018, but he played only nine games for the club. He returned to Uzbekistan in 2019 to join Pakhtakor, where in two years he won the league twice and the domestic cup once. He has attempted to find success abroad on several occasions, with clubs in the United Arab Emirates and Qatar, but has always returned to Pakhtakor and since 2022 he has stayed there. His involvement in the national team has, like much of his career, been inconsistent but he was back in the squad in late 2025 and has so far stayed there. "Every fan and every player in Uzbekistan lives with this desire,” he said in 2016. “I hope that I can make it to the national team and contribute to the team's qualification for the World Cup."

data.name: Abdulla Abdullaev
data.description: An experienced, tactically disciplined and versatile performer who can do a job in defence and midfield, Abdullaev has spent the bulk of his club career in Uzbekistan, apart from spells with two Emirati clubs: Dibba, whom he joined last year, and Khor Fakkan in 2023. When the Olmaliq-based club AGMK sold him to Khor Fakkan it was reported as the first overseas transfer in the club’s history, and earned them €200,000. At international level, he worked his way through the under-21s and under-23s before a senior debut in September 2021 against Sweden. “It went very well, we followed the instructions of the coach,” he said after the 2-1 defeat. “I tried to justify the faith he has shown in me. Of course there were shortcomings, but this was a real education. This match was a big lesson for me.”

data.name: Azizjon Ganiev
data.description: At 27, nine years after he made his full international debut and 11 after he played his first league game for Nasaf at the tender age of 16, Ganiev is hardly an unknown. But his presence as a key member of the World Cup squad is extraordinary given that of the 14 qualifying games that carried them to this tournament he played not a single minute, in fact received not one call-up. Since 2020 he has played his club football in the United Arab Emirates, first with Shabab al-Ahli, where he won a league title and several cups but also suffered two serious knee injuries, before in 2024 he moved to Al-Bataeh. Though he has spent most of his career in the centre of midfield he has been used increasingly in recent seasons, and particularly since his international re-emergence, as an inverted winger on the right.

data.name: Azizbek Amonov
data.description: One of a few players born in the Navoi region, not that conditions there sound ideal for the production of top-level talent. "I became interested in football in the fourth grade,” Amonov says. “When I was young, we played football around the bus stop, on the concrete pavement. Training sessions were held in such conditions." From the bus stop emerged an attacking midfielder and winger, who began his professional career at Lokomotiv Tashkent, where he won domestic league and cup titles. In 2022, he moved to Esteghlal, one of the most famous clubs in Iran, where he again won domestic league and cup titles. He has been back in Uzbekistan since 2024, and is currently with Dinamo. He made his debut for the national team in 2021 in a match against Sweden, but has not played in successive matches since 2022 – he was in the squad for the 2024 Asian Cup but returned without playing. "It doesn't matter who plays, the most important thing is the team's victory," he said.

data.name: Igor Sergeev
data.description: Sergeev is the top scorer in the history of Pakhtakor, Uzbekistan’s most successful club, having scored 145 goals in 262 matches for the Lions at the time of writing, winning the league five times and the cup three times. He was also the league’s top scorer in two seasons. Now 33, his career has taken him to clubs in China, the United Arab Emirates, Kazakhstan and Thailand. He won the Kazakh league with Tobol, and the Thai League Cup with Pathum United. In the 2024 Asian Cup in Qatar he tore his knee ligaments in a match against Australia. Many thought his career was over but in 2025 he re-signed for Pakhtakor and that year scored 20 goals in the Uzbek championship. After his first goal after his injury, the player celebrated the goal by kissing that knee. "You can't buy the emotions after a victory in football for any money," he said. In January Sergeev moved to another new country when he signed for the Iranian club Persepolis, though competition was halted by the outbreak of war a month later.

data.name: Abbosbek Fayzullaev
data.description: At the age of 22 Fayzullaev already has two Uzbek player of the year awards, having made his league debut aged just 17. As a child he moved from his family home in Syrdarya to Tashkent to join the Pakhtakor academy, where his uncle took him in and raised him as his own. His uncle died in 2021, the year Fayzullaev made his first-team debut, and the player still celebrates goals with a finger pointed to the heavens in tribute. "Every time I go on to the pitch I look at the stands, hoping that my uncle will come, but unfortunately that is no longer possible," he has said. Fayzullaev's wonderful free-kick against Syria at the 2023 Under-20 Asian Cup made him much more widely known in Uzbekistan and elsewhere, and in August of that year he moved to CSKA Moscow. “Many in Uzbekistan said: ‘He won’t be able to do it there, he’ll be back soon,’” he said. “Even some coaches expressed the same opinion. That gave me more motivation. I think I’ve proven myself.” Across two years in Russia he won the national cup and the super cup, and in Istanbul Basaksehir made him the most expensive player in their history.

data.name: Sherzod Esanov
data.description: Still only 23, Esanov has already been on a journey with his club football. After spells in Russia with Akron Tolyatti and Belarus with Dynamo Brest, he returned to Uzbekistan with Bukhara earlier this year. He was part of the Uzbekistan Under-20s side that won the Asian Cup in 2023. An energetic, box-to-box midfielder, he spoke of his pride at making this summer’s World Cup squad soon after arriving in the United States: “These feelings are difficult to express in words. Playing in the World Cup is the dream of every player. I’m learning a lot, especially on the tactical side, and I feel I’m growing physically. I think this is the case with all players. Now we just need the fans to back us. We will give our lives to play for our people.”

data.name: Bekhruz Karimov
data.description: Karimov was born in 2007 in the Namangan region to a father whoe was a builder abroad and a mother who was a school teacher. "I've been playing football since I was six. When I was in high school, I was rejected by Navbahor’s under-19 team because I was too young. After that incident, I started trying to prove that age is just a number," Karimov recalls. At 18 he continues to do so, his trajectory having been transformed within 12 extraordinary months of his league debut in March 2025. He scored his first league goal that October – and his second 20 minutes later – was named in the league’s young team of the year in December, played in the Under-23 Asian Cup in January, and made his full international debut in March. In April a broken toe threatened to stall his progress, but swift surgery has allowed him to return in time to participate in the World Cup, something that would have been unimaginable just over a year ago. “After the injury I was very depressed, but everyone around me supported me. This also helped me get back on my feet faster,” he said.

data.name: Avazbek Ulmasaliev
data.description: A solid, no-nonsense centre-back, Ulmasaliev has spent his whole club career in Uzbekistan, moving to AGMK in 2024. He is one of several surprise picks for the final World Cup squad – his international experience has so far been largely limited to Uunder-23 level and he didn’t feature during qualifying. But he did impress during Fabio Cannavaro’s experimental training camp in January when the head coach called in fringe players for assessment. Ulmasaliev said after the camp: “Everyone wanted to show what they were capable of. The friendly games were good and I was able to score my first goal. The training sessions under Cannavaro were very high-quality, and the team atmosphere was excellent. A call-up to the national team is every footballer's dream. This training camp showed that everyone has a chance to get noticed.”

data.name: Jakhongir Urozov
data.description: Jakhongir began his professional career at the Bunyodkor academy and made his debut in the first team in 2022. Tall and imposing, it did not take him long to make an impression, and the following year he was transferred to the Turkish club Eyupspor, where he won promotion under Arda Turan. "He is wonderful as a person,” Urosov said. “As a coach, he is very demanding, organised and attentive." He returned to Uzbekistan in 2025 to join Dinamo, where he is one of the key defensive figures. Andrey Pyatnitsky, the former Russia and Uzbekistan dual international who coached at Dinamo, has praised Urozov as “a national team-level player” but also identified a “concentration issue” that can affect his play. Despite being a defender, Urozov can pose a threat in set pieces in front of the opponents' goal, and demonstrated this on his debut, when he scored in a 3-1 win over Gabon.
